items.q: What does the 'S' in SPORT stand for?
items.correct: Specificity - training should match the demands of the sport.
items.why: Students guess fitness components. SPORT = Specificity, Progression, Overload, Reversibility, Tedium (or Time). Specificity means training matches what the athlete needs (sprinter doesn't train like a marathoner).

items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.correct: Information the performer senses from their own body during/after movement.
items.why: Students reverse the meaning. Intrinsic = from WITHIN (kinesthetic awareness, feel); extrinsic = from OUTSIDE (coach feedback, video, statistics). Experienced performers rely more on intrinsic feedback.

items.q: Which theory says performance rises in a straight line with arousal?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Drive Theory. “Inverted U Hypothesis” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which personality type is most likely to prefer archery?
items.why: It's Introvert. “Extrovert” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many main personality types do GCSE PE psychologists usually identify?
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
